blocks.body: Second Conditional shows an imagined or unlikely present or future situation with if + past, would + base verb.

{"level":"B1-B2","intro":"Use the Second Conditional for imagined, unreal, or unlikely situations in the present or future.","rules":[{"label":"CONDITION","color":"emerald","text":"The if-clause usually uses Past Simple or were.","example":"If I had more free time, I would learn Italian."},{"label":"RESULT","color":"blue","text":"The main clause most often uses would + base verb.","example":"If we lived by the sea, we would swim every day."},{"label":"DISTANCE FROM REALITY","color":"amber","text":"The past form here does not make the meaning past. It creates distance from the fact.","example":"If I were you, I would wait."}]}

{"title":"1. Form","intro":"The classic pattern is if + past simple, would + base verb.","items":[{"label":"Main pattern","title":"if + past, would + V","subtitle":"If I knew the answer, I would tell you."},{"label":"Were instead of was","title":"If I were you","subtitle":"If I were you, I would apologise."},{"label":"Result first","title":"would + V + if + clause","subtitle":"I would travel more if I had enough money."},{"label":"Other modals","title":"could / might + V","subtitle":"If she practised more, she could win."}]}

{"title":"2. Main uses","sections":[{"label":"Imagined present","color":"emerald","description":"The situation is not real now, but we imagine it and describe the possible result.","examples":[{"en":"If I had a car, I would drive to work.","ua":"If I had a car, I would drive to work."},{"en":"If she lived closer, we would see her more often.","ua":"If she lived closer, we would see her more often."}]},{"label":"Unlikely future","color":"blue","description":"We talk about a future situation that feels unlikely or very hypothetical.","examples":[{"en":"If he won the lottery, he would buy a house in Spain.","ua":"If he won the lottery, he would buy a house in Spain."},{"en":"If they moved abroad, they would need better English.","ua":"If they moved abroad, they would need better English."}]},{"label":"Advice through distance","color":"amber","description":"If I were you often introduces soft advice.","examples":[{"en":"If I were you, I would talk to her directly.","ua":"If I were you, I would talk to her directly."},{"en":"If I were in your position, I would wait one more day.","ua":"If I were in your position, I would wait one more day."}]}]}

{"title":"3. Common mistakes","items":[{"label":"Mistake 1","color":"rose","title":"Putting would in the if-clause.","wrong":"If I would have more time, I would read more.","right":"If I had more time, I would read more.","hint":"In the Second Conditional, would normally belongs in the main clause, not after if."},{"label":"Mistake 2","color":"amber","title":"Treating the past form as real past time.","wrong":"If I knew her number yesterday, I would call now.","right":"If I knew her number, I would call her now.","hint":"The past form here shows unreality, not past time."},{"label":"Mistake 3","color":"sky","title":"Avoiding were in If I were you.","wrong":"If I was you, I would leave.","right":"If I were you, I would leave.","hint":"For advice and more standard English, were is the usual choice."}]}

{"title":"4. Quick rule","items":["Use if + past, would + base verb for an imagined present or unlikely future situation.","The past form in the if-clause shows distance from reality, not past time.","If I were you is a common advice formula.","The main clause can also use could or might instead of would."]}
